data.title: Targa Resources says it is well hedged against direct commodity price exposure
US Natural Gas Stocks Posts Smaller Decline Than Expected in Week Ended Feb. 13
Canada's Trade Deficit Balloons as Exports to U.S. Falter -- 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart


OTTAWA--Canada's trade deficit inflated sharply last year after exports were slugged by U.S tariffs and heightened uncertainty.

The country recorded a yearly merchandise-trade shortfall of 31.18 billion Canadian dollars, the equivalent of about US$22.77 billion, the widest on record outside of the first year of the pandemic, Statistics Canada said Thursday. Despite a recovery in the final month of 2025, exports dipped 0.2% annually, and would have been down a sharper 3% if not for a jump in precious-metals sales and a surge in the price of gold.

The driver of the trade gap was a pullback in shipments to the U.S., by far Canada's largest market, though that lessened in 2025 as trade activity further afield rose strongly. Exports to the U.S. sank 5.8% over the last year, outpacing a 2.9% decline in American imports that shrank the longstanding trade surplus with the U.S. to C$81.59 billion, the narrowest level since 2020.

Last year was a rocky one for Canada's economy, and in particular for manufacturing sectors most exposed to the Trump administration's tariffs and isolationist policy. Tariffs have weakened the country's economy and have helped spur what the central bank has described as a structural change on top of reduced immigration and an aging population.

The Bank of Canada has forecast exports will recover this year, though along a weaker path, and the economy should see modest growth after having likely stalled in the final quarter of 2025. Still, uncertainty lingers as the existing North American trade pact comes up for review. In an effort to pivot the country away from reliance on the U.S., Prime Minister Mark Carney is targeting a doubling of non-U.S. exports over the next decade.

There were early signs Carney has had some success. Exports to countries other than the U.S. climbed 17.2% in 2025, while imports from non-U.S. countries increased 12.4%. The proportion of Canada's good exports headed to the U.S. fell to 71.1% for the year from almost 76% in 2024, and the share of imports from the neighboring country declined to 58.8% from slightly more than 62%.

Trade volumes stabilized through the second half of last year after falling steeply in the spring with the Trump administration's threatened and imposed tariffs on Canada and sectors important to its economy, such as autos and steel. A 2.1% rise in export volumes in the final quarter of last year, and a slight 0.3% rise in imports, suggest net trade will offer a slight lift to gross domestic product for the quarter.

"That doesn't mean that exports are recovering particularly strongly from the declines seen over the spring," Andrew Grantham, senior economist at CIBC Capital Markets, said.

Grantham said there remains great divergence by sector, with exports of products hit hardest by U.S. tariffs generally still depressed. That, he said, highlights the importance of renegotiating the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement and maintaining the tariff exemption for products covered by trade deal.

For the final month of 2025, Canadian exports increased 2.6% and imports nudged up 0.6%. That halved the merchandise-trade deficit to C$1.31 billion, which is narrower than the C$2.1 billion economists had anticipated, and it comes after the deficit widened sharply the month before to C$2.59 billion.

Monthly exports to the U.S. were up 1.1% after declining two months straight, while shipments to countries beyond the U.S. increased 5.8% to hit an all-time high.

The recovery in exports for the month was driven in part by an 18.0% jump in metals and minerals, which rebounded from a sharp pullback in November, boosted by a jump in volatile gold shipments to the U.K. Exports of aircraft were also up sharply for the latest month, boosted by large shipments of business jets to the U.S. at the end of the year.

Excluding shipments of metal and mineral products, exports in December dipped 0.2%.

"Significant trade uncertainty remains...but we continue to expect, as a base-case, that a more stable international trade backdrop in 2026 will leave trade as less of a headwind to growth than it was in 2025," Nathan Janzen, Royal Bank of Canada assistant chief economist, said.


Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-19-26 1153ET

Wall St dips on mixed economic data, US-Iran tensions boost crude, gold
data.description:
NEW YORK, Feb 19 (Reuters) - U.S. stocks softened on Thursday following mixed economic data and Walmart's disappointing coming-year forecast, while signs of a sturdy labor market helped the dollar extend its upward climb. 

All three major U.S. stock indexes followed their European counterparts modestly lower, setting course to snap their three-session winning streaks, while mounting concerns over oil supply arising from a potential U.S.-Iran conflict helped bolster crude and gold prices.

"Today (investors are) weighing of some of the economic data and what Walmart's earnings are saying in terms of the consumer," says Chuck Carlson, CEO at Horizon Investment Services in Hammond, Indiana.

"I think investors are trying to grapple with trying to make a decision whether this rotation trade has legs or if it's starting to run its course," Carlson added, referring to the transition away from mega-cap tech-related momentum stocks.

A spate of economic data showed a dip in jobless claims and U.S. trade data, which recorded its widest goods deficit record as imports rebounded despite President Donald Trump's tariffs.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 121.37 points, or 0.24%, to 49,542.26, the S&P 500 fell 13.27 points, or 0.19%, to 6,868.52 and the Nasdaq Composite fell 43.72 points, or 0.18%, to 22,712.95. 

European shares retreated from Wednesday's record closing high as investors parsed a mixed bag of corporate earnings, particularly Airbus and iron ore producer Rio Tinto.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe fell 2.21 points, or 0.21%, to 1,047.48.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index fell 0.62%, while Europe's broad FTSEurofirst 300 index fell 16.37 points, or 0.65%.

Emerging market stocks rose 0.77 points, or 0.05%, to 1,561.65. MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan closed higher by 0.35% to 800.35, while Japan's Nikkei rose 323.99 points, or 0.57%, to 57,467.83.

U.S.-IRAN TENSIONS SEND OIL PRICES HIGHER

Crude prices continued to rise on supply concerns arising from the growing possibility of a potential military conflict between the United States and Iran.

"It's the elephant in the room, what's going on in the Middle East," Carlson said. "It certainly has ramifications for commodities, specifically oil and also gold."

U.S. crude rose 2.06% to $66.53 a barrel and Brent rose to $71.58 per barrel, up 1.79% on the day.

The dollar continued its climb as solid jobless claims data and minutes from the U.S. Federal Reserve appeared to show a divide among policymakers regarding the need to cut interest rates in the near term.

    "The jobs market is okay, but there's still a bit of job insecurity that's out there," Carlson added. "It gives the Fed a pause, trying to evaluate not just the numbers, but how job insecurity is affecting the rest of the economy."

The dollar index, which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, rose 0.14% to 97.84, with the euro down 0.09% at $1.1771.

Against the Japanese yen, the dollar strengthened 0.11% to 154.99.

In cryptocurrencies, bitcoin fell 0.07% to $66,274.64. Ethereum declined 1.07% to $1,920.86.

U.S. Treasury yields edged higher as traders evaluated the Fed's path forward regarding interest rate cuts ahead of the Treasury Department's $9 billion auction of 30-year debt.

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes rose 0.9 basis points to 4.09%, from 4.081% late on Wednesday.

The 30-year bond yield rose 1.4 basis points to 4.7206% from 4.707% late on Wednesday.

The 2-year note yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate expectations for the Federal Reserve, rose 1.2 basis points to 3.472%, from 3.46% late on Wednesday.

Gold prices increased on renewed demand for the safe-haven metal as investors monitored U.S.-Iran tensions, while jobless claims data hinted at labor market stability.

Spot gold rose 0.69% to $5,013.54 an ounce. U.S. gold futures fell 0.04% to $4,984.50 an ounce.

(Reporting by Stephen Culp; Additional reporting by Harry Robertson in London; Editing by Nia Williams)

By Stephen Culp

data.title: Ferrari Board Proposes 21% Increase in Dividend
data.description:
(Alliance News) - Ferrari NV announced Thursday that its board of directors will propose to shareholders a dividend of EUR3.615 per ordinary share, representing an increase of approximately 21% compared to the previous year, for a total distribution of around EUR640 million.

The proposal will now be submitted to the shareholders' meeting, scheduled to take place on April 15.

If approved, the ex-dividend date will be set for April 20 on the EXM in Italy and April 21 on the NYSE in the USA. The record date is scheduled for April 21, 2026, for ordinary shares traded on both exchanges.

The payment date will be May 5.

By Michele Cirulli, Alliance News Reporter

Comments and questions to redazione@alliancenews.com

Copyright 2026 Alliance News IS Italian Service Ltd. All rights reserved.
